title: The Urgent Need to Better Integrate Neuroscience and Neuroethics.
abstract: An entire chapter of the BRAIN 2.0 Neuroethics roadmap, and three articles in this special issue (Chiong 2020; Goering and Klein 2020; Salles and Farisco 2020), focus on the topic of integrating neuroscience and neuroethics. This commentary builds upon the insightful suggestions put forth by the roadmap and the three abovementioned articles. First, I argue that any solution to better integration should be grounded in a better understanding of the nature of the divide itself. To that end, I offer initial observations regarding the nature of the rift, specifically with regard to neuroscientists’ perceptions of neuroethics, and suggest that additional work can be conducted to address this issue empirically. Second, I concur with the need for increased funding for training programs and centers of excellence, and add that integration should be bidirectional: instead of just focusing on ethics training for neuroscientists, we should consider how ethicists without prior neuroscience experience can gain more empirical and experiential knowledge of the field, as doing so can enhance the robustness of both domains.
